title: CopyEngineeringConductorOutput
description: Output schema for the Copy Engineering Conductor evaluation report.

behavioral_verdict.description: Whether the copy moves the prospect closer to, away from, or neither toward the desired behavior.

score.description: Overall behavioral movement score. Derived from the weighted average of force scores. Not a quality score — a behavioral movement score.

primary_reason.description: One paragraph. The main reason for the verdict. Not a list.

critical_bottleneck.description: One sentence. The single force most blocking action right now. Not the lowest score — the force whose absence prevents downstream movement.

highest_leverage_fix.description: One sentence. The single change that would most increase the probability of the desired behavior. Format: 'The highest-leverage fix is to strengthen [force] because [reason].'

rewrite_direction.description: Two to four sentences. What the next version must accomplish behaviorally — not how to write it, but what behavioral job it must do.

risk_flags.description: List of fabrication, trust, clarity, or compliance risks. Empty array if none identified.

final_recommendation.description: Terminal verdict. Ship = copy is ready. Revise = fix the bottleneck. Rebuild = structural problem that cannot be fixed with edits.

final_recommendation_reason.description: One sentence explaining the final recommendation.

reason.description: One sentence explaining why this force received this score.

examples.primary_reason: The copy correctly names the problem and offers a plausible solution, but fails to generate belief that this specific provider can deliver the result — leaving the prospect informed but unmoved.

attention.reason: Opening line creates pattern interrupt for the right audience.

curiosity.reason: Promise of a 'copy audit' is intriguing but undefined — reader doesn't know what they'll get.

understanding.reason: Offer is clear: a free 30-minute call.

desire.reason: Names the pain (ads not converting) but doesn't paint the desired future state.

belief.reason: No proof, no credentials, no case studies — claim that 'we fix what's broken' is unsubstantiated.

trust.reason: No social proof, no founder name, no company background visible on this page.

friction_reduction.reason: Free offer and 30-minute time limit reduce risk, but 'no pitch' claim is not believable without context.

action.reason: CTA is clear but not compelling — 'Book Now' does not sell the value of the call.

loyalty.reason: No signal of ongoing relationship or what happens after the call.

advocacy.reason: Nothing in the copy would cause a prospect to share or recommend.

examples.critical_bottleneck: Belief — the copy makes a strong claim with zero evidence, which stops the prospect from moving from interest to intent.
examples.highest_leverage_fix: The highest-leverage fix is to strengthen Belief by adding one specific, verifiable result achieved for a named client type before the CTA.
examples.rewrite_direction: The next version must give the prospect one reason to believe the claim before asking them to act. This does not require a full case study — a single specific outcome ('We rewrote the homepage for a Series A SaaS tool and their trial signups increased 40% in 30 days') does more behavioral work than any amount of confident positioning language.
risk_flags: [RISK] 'fix the part that's actually broken' — implies diagnostic certainty that is unsupported; needs evidence or softening.
[RISK] 'no pitch' — this claim is commonly disbelieved; requires proof or removal.
examples.final_recommendation_reason: The structure is sound but the belief gap will prevent most solution-aware prospects from booking.
